claim: Terminal is the ideal form factor for LLM-based coding because it maps naturally to how developers work, requires minimal new UI abstractions, and integrates directly into existing workflows
actionable: When building coding agents, consider CLI/terminal as a primary interface rather than web UI to maximize developer adoption and integration

claim: Claude Code's development workflow has evolved from single-line completions to file-level changes to task-level changes, with future iterations moving toward project-level orchestration
actionable: Design coding agents to work at progressively higher levels of abstraction - aim for project-level planning and multi-agent orchestration as models improve

claim: Prompting in the terminal (with input outlines and slash commands) combined with settings configuration creates a powerful architecture for controlling agentic behavior
actionable: Use slash commands, prompting, and settings.json/CLAUDE.md configuration to structure agentic coding workflows

claim: Subagents in Claude Code enable delegation of tasks to specialized agents with different configurations, improving code quality and modularity
actionable: Create subagents for specific tasks (deep thinking, code review, refactoring) with custom prompts and settings

claim: Non-technical product designers can use Claude Code to prototype and push designs to production, unlocking new skills for non-engineers without traditional coding training
actionable: Enable designers to use Claude Code for prototyping and design tweaks; pair with engineers for complex architecture changes

claim: Claude Code's minimal UI design principle keeps the interface clean and lets the model shine; personality touches (reticulating, thinking mode indicators) improve UX without clutter
actionable: When designing agent interfaces, minimize UI elements and let the model do the work; add personality touches for clarity, not decoration
